title: Pricing the Slow Storm
subtitle: How climate feeds every other risk

hook.metaphor: Climate risk rarely arrives wearing a label that says 'climate risk'. It shows up disguised — as a loan that defaults, a property that's repriced, a factory that floods, an insurance claim that spikes. It's a slow storm that seeps into all the *other* risks you've already learned. The skill is recognising its fingerprints where they hide.
hook.scenario: A bank's loan book looks fine on traditional credit measures. But many borrowers are farmers in a drought-prone region and businesses on a flood plain. Today's numbers don't show it — but climate risk is quietly building inside what looks like ordinary credit risk. How would the bank ever see it coming?

learn: Climate risk is a **risk multiplier**, not a standalone silo. It seeps into: **credit risk** (borrowers hit by drought or floods default — our farmers), **market risk** (carbon-heavy assets get repriced as the transition bites), **operational risk** (your own sites flood or burn), and **insurance/underwriting risk** (claims rise, some risks become uninsurable).

The challenge is that **traditional, backward-looking measures miss it.** A credit model trained on the past won't see a flood plain's future risk; today's numbers look fine right up until the slow storm lands. You can't manage climate risk with a rear-view mirror.

The main tool is **climate scenario analysis**: modelling how the business fares under different futures — e.g. an orderly transition, a disorderly one, or a 'hot house' world of high physical risk. It's forward-looking by design, asking 'how would our loan book / assets / operations hold up under each path?' (the stress-testing mindset from Market and Liquidity risk, stretched over decades).

Regulators now expect this: Australia's **APRA CPG 229** guides banks and insurers on climate scenario analysis, and central banks worldwide run climate stress tests. The message: prove you've looked *forward* at climate, not just backward at history.

And the data is genuinely hard — long horizons, deep uncertainty, sparse history. So the goal isn't false precision but **direction and preparedness**: knowing where your concentrations of climate-exposed risk sit (drought-region farmers, flood-plain businesses, carbon-heavy holdings) and acting before the rear-view mirror finally shows the crash.
bridge: So at work, you can hunt for climate risk where it actually hides — inside credit, market, operational, and insurance exposures — rather than waiting for a neat 'climate' label. Ask: *where are our concentrations of climate-exposed risk, and have we looked forward (scenario analysis) rather than just back?* The slow storm is invisible in today's numbers precisely until it isn't.

interactions.title: Climate hiding inside which risk?
interactions.prompt: Sort each climate-driven loss by the 'other' risk it shows up as.

items.label: Drought-hit farmers default on their loans
A bushfire shuts your own factory
A 'stranded' coal holding loses market value

interactions.title: The fine-looking loan book
interactions.prompt: Traditional credit measures look healthy, but borrowers cluster in drought and flood zones. How does the bank get ahead of it?

options.label: Trust the current numbers — they look fine.
options.feedback: Backward-looking measures are exactly what miss climate risk. Today's healthy numbers won't show the drought/flood concentration until the slow storm lands and it's too late.

options.label: Run forward-looking climate scenario analysis on the book.
options.feedback: Exactly. Scenario analysis asks how the loan book holds up under different climate futures — surfacing the drought/flood concentration that historical metrics hide. Look forward, not just back.

options.label: Wait until defaults actually rise, then react.
options.feedback: Reacting after the storm forfeits all the cheap options to adjust. The whole point is to see the building risk early, via forward-looking analysis.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Climate risk hides inside the other risks — defaulting farmers (credit), repriced carbon assets (market), flooded factories (operational) — and since backward-looking numbers miss it, you use forward-looking scenario analysis to see the slow storm coming.”

checkpoint.q: Climate risk is best understood as…
options: A standalone risk unrelated to others
A multiplier that seeps into credit, market, operational, and insurance risk
checkpoint.explain: It shows up disguised as defaults, repricings, floods, and claims — inside the other risks.

checkpoint.q: Why do traditional risk measures struggle with climate risk?
options: They're backward-looking and miss building future exposures
checkpoint.explain: A rear-view mirror won't show a flood plain's future risk — you need forward-looking analysis.

checkpoint.q: Climate scenario analysis is valuable because it…
options: Models how the business fares under different climate futures, forward-looking
checkpoint.explain: It tests resilience across paths (orderly/disorderly transition, hot-house world) rather than trusting history.
